beginner.question: What is HTML and what does it stand for?
beginner.answer: HTML stands for HyperText Markup Language. It is the standard markup language used to create and structure content on the web.

beginner.question: What is the difference between HTML and a programming language?
beginner.answer: HTML is a markup language, not a programming language. It uses tags to structure content, whereas programming languages (like JavaScript or Python) involve logic, variables, loops, and functions to perform computations.

beginner.question: What is the basic structure of an HTML document?
beginner.answer: The basic structure includes <!DOCTYPE html>, <html>, <head> (with <meta> and <title>), and <body> tags.

beginner.question: What does the <title> tag do?
beginner.answer: The <title> tag defines the page title that appears in the browser tab. It is also used by search engines as the clickable headline in search results.

beginner.question: What is a tag and what is an element in HTML?
beginner.answer:
A tag is the markup syntax between < > brackets (e.g.,

). An element includes the opening tag, content, and closing tag (e.g.,

Hello

).

beginner.question: What is the difference between <head> and <body>?
beginner.answer: The <head> contains metadata, title, and links to stylesheets — not displayed on the page. The <body> contains all visible content that users see.

beginner.question: How do you view the HTML source of a webpage?
beginner.answer: Right-click anywhere on the page and select 'View Page Source' (or press Ctrl+U on Windows).

beginner.question: What is a self-closing tag? Give an example.
beginner.answer:
A self-closing (void) tag has no content and no closing tag, like
for a line break or for an image.

beginner.question: What does the <!DOCTYPE html> declaration do?
beginner.answer: It tells the browser that the document is written in HTML5 and triggers standards mode rendering.

beginner.question: Can you have multiple <h1> tags on a page?
beginner.answer: Technically yes, but it is best practice to use only one <h1> per page for SEO and accessibility reasons.

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of semantic HTML elements?
intermediate.answer: Semantic elements (like <article>, <nav>, <header>) clearly describe their meaning in a human- and machine-readable way. They improve accessibility, SEO, and code maintainability.

intermediate.question: Explain the DOM (Document Object Model).
intermediate.answer: The DOM is a programming interface for HTML documents. It represents the page as a tree of nodes and objects, allowing JavaScript to dynamically access and update content, structure, and styles.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between HTML4 and HTML5?
intermediate.answer: HTML5 introduced semantic elements (<header>, <nav>, <article>, <section>, <footer>), native audio/video support (<audio>, <video>), canvas for graphics, form input types (email, date, etc.), and local storage APIs.

intermediate.question: What is the viewport meta tag and why is it important?
intermediate.answer: The viewport meta tag (<meta name='viewport' content='width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0'>) controls the layout on mobile browsers, ensuring the page scales correctly and is responsive.

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of the alt attribute on images?
intermediate.answer: The alt attribute provides alternative text for images. It is used by screen readers for accessibility and displayed when the image fails to load. It also improves SEO.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between absolute and relative URLs in HTML?
intermediate.answer: An absolute URL includes the full path (https://example.com/page.html). A relative URL points to a resource relative to the current document (/images/photo.jpg).

intermediate.question: How does browser rendering work for an HTML page?
intermediate.answer: The browser parses HTML into a DOM tree, then parses CSS into the CSSOM. These are combined into the render tree, and the browser paints the pixels to the screen.

intermediate.question: What are void elements in HTML? Give three examples.
intermediate.answer:
Void elements are elements that cannot have child content and do not need a closing tag. Examples:
, , ,
, .

intermediate.question: What is the difference between <div> and <span>?
intermediate.answer: <div> is a block-level element that takes up the full width, creating line breaks before and after. <span> is an inline element that wraps only the content without breaking the flow.

intermediate.question: Why is it important to validate HTML code?
intermediate.answer: Validation ensures your HTML follows W3C standards, which leads to better cross-browser compatibility, improved accessibility, and easier maintenance.

advanced.question: Explain the concept of the critical rendering path. What is the role of HTML in it?
advanced.answer: The critical rendering path is the sequence of steps the browser takes to convert HTML, CSS, and JavaScript into pixels on the screen. HTML is parsed first to build the DOM, which blocks rendering until the CSSOM is also built. HTML delivery and parsing speed directly affect perceived performance.

advanced.question: How does HTML interact with the Shadow DOM?
advanced.answer: The Shadow DOM is a browser technology that encapsulates a DOM subtree within a component, isolating it from the main document. HTML templates (<template>) and custom elements use Shadow DOM to create encapsulated, reusable components.

advanced.question: What is the difference between HTML parsing and XHTML parsing?
advanced.answer: HTML parsing is error-tolerant and will attempt to render even malformed documents. XHTML parsing (using application/xhtml+xml MIME type) is strict and follows XML rules — any syntax error causes the browser to stop processing and display an error page.

advanced.question: Explain the purpose of the <head> section in terms of SEO, performance, and social sharing.
advanced.answer: The <head> contains meta tags for SEO (<meta name='description'>, <meta name='robots'>), Open Graph tags for social sharing (<meta property='og:title'>), and performance hints (<link rel='preload'>, <link rel='preconnect'>). It also links to stylesheets and sets the page title.

advanced.question: What is the difference between localStorage and sessionStorage in HTML5?
advanced.answer: localStorage stores data with no expiration — it persists even after the browser is closed. sessionStorage stores data only for the current session and is cleared when the tab is closed. Both store data as key-value pairs and are part of the Web Storage API.

advanced.question: How do HTML, CSS, and JavaScript interact in a modern single-page application (SPA)?
advanced.answer: In an SPA, HTML provides the initial shell (often a minimal index.html with a root <div>), CSS handles styling, and JavaScript dynamically manipulates the DOM and fetches data via AJAX to render views without full page reloads, often using frameworks like React, Vue, or Angular.

advanced.question: What are the W3C and WHATWG, and how do they relate to HTML standards?
advanced.answer: The W3C (World Wide Web Consortium) is the main international standards organization for the web. WHATWG (Web Hypertext Application Technology Working Group) was formed in 2004 by browser vendors to address HTML development more quickly. In 2019, they agreed to collaborate on a single Living Standard for HTML.

advanced.question: How does the <template> element work in HTML?
advanced.answer: The <template> element holds HTML that is not rendered immediately. Its content is a separate DocumentFragment that can be cloned and inserted into the DOM via JavaScript, useful for creating reusable component markup.

advanced.question: Explain the role of the HTML parser in the browser. How does it handle script tags?
advanced.answer: The HTML parser reads the HTML byte stream and constructs the DOM tree. When it encounters a <script> tag (without async or defer), it blocks parsing, fetches and executes the script, then resumes. Async scripts download in parallel and execute as soon as available, while defer scripts execute in order after parsing completes.

advanced.question: What are web components and how are they built with standard HTML features?
advanced.answer: Web components are a set of browser APIs that allow developers to create reusable custom elements. They are built using three technologies: Custom Elements (define new HTML tags via JavaScript), Shadow DOM (encapsulated DOM tree), and HTML Templates (<template> element for markup).
